Journal/Essay

Recognition is not a points problem. It's a language problem.

The industry spent fifteen years gamifying gratitude and we ended up with leaderboards for who said "nice work" the most. Here's what our analysis of 82,000 kudos suggests we should do instead.

N
Nadia Okonkwo CEO & co-founder
Feb 14, 2025 12 min read

Somewhere between 2008 and 2014, the entire employee-recognition industry decided that the problem with saying thank you at work was that we didn't have a scoreboard. The prevailing wisdom — rolled into dozens of products I've now personally deployed and uninstalled — was that recognition was an activity you wanted more of. And if you wanted more of something, well, you gamified it. Streaks. Badges. Leaderboards for the people who gave the most points. A marketplace where those points could be spent on an Amazon gift card, a yoga mat, or, if enough people recognized you, a weighted blanket.

I know because I ran People at a Series C fintech and rolled out three of these tools in four years. They all had the same lifecycle: breathless launch, six months of activity, eight months of slow decline, a year of people quietly not using it, and then me, the VP of People, going to the CHRO and saying "we need a new tool." It wasn't a new tool we needed. It was a new idea.

The thing we were optimizing was the wrong thing.

Here is what I believe now, having worked on recognition software for four years on the other side: the problem was never that people in companies weren't giving enough recognition. The problem was that the recognition they were giving was hollow.

The software we built — all of us, collectively, as an industry — was designed to increase the volume of recognition, because volume is what you can measure in a dashboard. More kudos this month than last. More points redeemed. More employees "engaged." But nobody was asking whether any of those kudos actually meant something to the person receiving them. Or, to put it more pointedly: whether the person giving them had actually paid attention.

"Nice work on the deck today!" is not the same thing as "The reason that deck landed is because you spent Tuesday night reworking slide 14, and it changed the whole narrative."
— Something I've said in every values workshop since 2021

The first sentence takes three seconds. The second takes maybe ninety. And the research on this — the good research, not the vendor-funded variety — is extremely clear: the second one is 40 times more effective at driving the outcomes that recognition is supposed to drive.

Not retention. Not engagement. I mean the actual outcome: a person at your company feels seen.

What we actually found when we read 82,000 kudos.

When we built Bryte Rewards, we made a deliberate choice early on to privilege the sentence over the points. Our recognition cards are big. They're editorial-looking. They have a lot of whitespace. You can see the text from ten feet away. This isn't decorative — it's a forcing function. If the text is small and the points are big, guess which one people optimize for.

Last fall, our research team — okay, one of our engineers on a weekend — ran a structured analysis of 82,184 kudos sent across our customer base over the prior twelve months. We looked at two things: (1) what the kudos actually said, and (2) what the recipient did next. Did they react? Did they share? Did they reciprocate in the following month? Did they, six months later, still be at the company?

47%
Of kudos were generic — "great job!", "thanks!", ≤6 words
3.2×
More reactions on kudos that named a specific moment
82%
Of "high-signal" kudos referenced a decision, not a deliverable

Let me unpack that last one, because it's the most interesting finding and the one that changed how we teach this. Bad recognition describes output. "Great deck." "Awesome demo." "Thanks for the PR." Good recognition describes a decision. "I saw you push back on the timeline and I think that was the right call." "You took the brief and made it sharper — I don't think we would have won the pitch on the original." "You stayed up late on something that wasn't glamorous and I want you to know I noticed."

Output is what someone produced. Decisions are what someone chose. And when you recognize a decision, you are doing something structurally different: you are telling the person that you are paying attention to their judgment, not their yield. You are telling them they are a thinking being whose calls you respect, not a pair of hands whose throughput you monitor.

The format we recommend now.

We teach a four-sentence structure in every onboarding we run. It's not a rule; it's a scaffold. It goes:

  1. Name the moment. "The Nordstrom pitch." Not "your work." Something concrete.
  2. Name the decision. "You pushed to reframe the narrative on slide 14."
  3. Name the effect. "It's the reason we won the room."
  4. Name the person. "That's your instinct, and I think you should trust it more."

You can write one of these in under two minutes. It is indistinguishable from something a close friend would say to you about your work. And the data suggests people will remember receiving one six months later, which is not something I'd have said about any of the kudos I sent or received in the products I used previously.

Try it yourself

Right now, pull up the last three kudos you sent. Could you swap the name on them and have them read the same? If yes — that's a generic. Rewrite one this week with the four-sentence scaffold and send it to that person. Report back.

Why the points thing actively makes this worse.

Once you start paying attention to this, the points systems look increasingly strange. Points flatten everything. A three-sentence "great job on the deck" and a ninety-sentence deeply-considered note about someone's growth over a year — they both route through the same "50 points" in your app. The points were supposed to be a unit of value, but they ended up being a unit of acknowledgement, and once you've rolled everything into one number, you've told your team that the number is the thing.

I'm not saying points are evil. We have a points system in our product. It's there because redeemable rewards are genuinely nice and people want them. But we deliberately keep it small on the card. Under the text. In smaller type. Because the text is the point. The points are the receipt.

The points are the receipt. The text is the thing.

What this means for People teams right now.

If you are reading this and you run People at a company, you probably have three options on the table:

  1. Keep your current recognition tool. Teach managers the four-sentence format. Measure quality, not volume. It will still be an uphill fight because the tool's UI is nudging everyone toward the hollow version — but it is absolutely possible.
  2. Switch tools. Pick something — and I will leave the list of options generous here, we are not the only one — that makes the sentence the main event. Ask your vendor how big the message is on the card, and listen to how they answer.
  3. Do nothing. This is fine, honestly. Bad recognition software is a net neutral at best. Your team is probably giving good recognition in chat DMs that you will never see on a dashboard, and that's fine too.

The one thing I would beg you not to do is to launch a new program aimed at increasing the number of kudos sent per employee per month. That metric is lying to you. It will go up and your people will not feel more seen, and six months from now you will be right back here, wondering why.

Recognition isn't a points problem. It never was. It's a language problem — a case where we made the easier, cheaper, more-trackable version of a thing into a ritual, and the ritual hollowed the original out until nobody quite remembered what we were doing to begin with.

Fix the language. The rest takes care of itself.

The sequel

Next month: the four-sentence scaffold, broken down — with 14 real examples from the data (names changed). Subscribe to the mailing list if you want it in your inbox.

Sharing this with your People team?

Nadia Okonkwo

CEO & co-founder, Bryte Rewards

Former VP People at a Series C fintech in Toronto. Spent 10+ years running People teams of 12 to 80. Deploys and uninstalls recognition software for a living. Writes about HR, values, and the things managers accidentally optimize for.
